title: The Patient Who Fell
oneLiner: Common cause of hospital attendance in older adults; high risk of serious injury, syncope, and functional decline.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Intracranial haemorrhage** (subdural/extradural after head strike) — especially on anticoagulation/antiplatelets. **Single finding:** deteriorating GCS or new unilateral fixed/dilated pupil, focal neurology.  
- **Cervical spine fracture** (elderly fall from standing, especially with head strike). **Single finding:** midline neck tenderness + neurological deficit (or documented trauma + altered consciousness).  
- **Hip fracture** (frequent in osteoporotic elderly). **Single finding:** shortened, externally rotated leg + inability to weight bear.  
- **Rhabdomyolysis** (prolonged lying on floor, e.g., >2 hours). **Single finding:** dark/cola-coloured urine + raised CK + rising creatinine.  
- **Cardiac cause of fall** (arrhythmia, myocardial infarction). **Single finding:** loss of consciousness + abnormal ECG (ST changes, AV block, atrial fibrillation).
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Found on floor; unable to get up; pain in hip/back/head; confusion; loss of consciousness (or witness report of collapse); headache, neck pain.  
- **Key exam findings:** GCS; visible head injury (scalp haematoma, periorbital ecchymosis); neck tenderness/step-off; shortened/externally rotated leg; limb weakness; external signs of fracture; urine colour (dark, decreased output); postural hypotension.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Syncope (cardiac/vasovagal/orthostatic)** → loss of consciousness + normal neurological exam; cardiac syncope often with chest pain or palpitations or ECG changes.  
- **Seizure** → tongue bite, incontinence, post-ictal confusion.  
- **Stroke/TIA** → sudden focal neurology (not necessarily fall alone).  
- **Mechanical fall** (trip/slip/obstacle) → no loss of consciousness, no prodrome.  
- **Acute illness** (UTI, sepsis, MI) → fever, hypotension, raised inflammatory markers, abnormal ECG.  
- **Medication side effect** (e.g., antihypertensives, sedatives) → postural hypotension, history of recent changes.  
- **Splitter:** Was there a clear loss of consciousness? If yes → cardiac/neurological cause (ECG/post-ictal); if no → mechanical or acute illness.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - ECG (**never miss** in any fall with loss of consciousness or age >60) – rule out arrhythmia, ischaemia, QT prolongation.  
  - Capillary blood glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia).  
  - Pulse oximetry (rule out hypoxia).  
  - Postural BP (lie, 1 min, 3 min) – if orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg with symptoms).  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, CRP, CK (rhabdomyolysis if prolonged lying), coagulation screen (if on anticoagulant), troponin (if syncope/cardiac concern).  
  - Group and save if hip fracture or head injury needing surgery.  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **CT head** (if: GCS <15 or new confusion after head injury; focal neurology; on anticoagulation/antiplatelet with head strike; clinical suspicion of subdural).  
  - **XR hip/pelvis** (if pain on hip rotation, inability to weight bear, shortened/rotated leg).  
  - **C-spine CT** (if high-risk: age >65, dangerous mechanism, neck pain/tenderness, neurological symptoms, or if head CT already needed).  
- **Special tests:**  
  - If cardiac syncope suspected and ECG normal → consider echo (structural disease), 24h tape (arrhythmia), tilt table (if recurrent).  
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** in any fall with loss of consciousness – omission risks missing occult MI or arrhythmia.  
  - Also: **CT head** in anyone on anticoagulation with head strike, even if asymptomatic.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - ABCDE approach, spinal precautions if c-spine suspicion; high-flow O₂ if trauma/respiratory compromise.  
  - Reverse anticoagulation if acute intracranial bleed: prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) + vitamin K (for warfarin) – dose per local protocol; for DOACs, specific reversal agents ([check local policy]).  
  - Analgesia for injury: paracetamol 1g IV/PO (avoid NSAIDs if bleeding risk or renal impairment).  
  - Fluid resuscitation if hypotensive (crystalloid 500 mL bolus, reassess).  
  - If hip fracture: gentle traction, orthopaedic referral, NBM if surgical plan.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Falls assessment: medication review (stop unnecessary sedatives, antihypertensives), orthostatic BP, vision/hearing check, gait and balance assessment.  
  - Orthogeriatric input for hip fracture (can't weight bear, surgical candidate).  
  - Monitor neuro observations (GCS, pupil size, limb power) if head injury – frequency per local protocol (e.g., q15min for first 2h, then q30min if stable).  
  - CK and renal function monitoring if rhabdomyolysis: IV fluids (aim for urine output >2 mL/kg/hr if CK >5000 IU/L).  
  - Bone protection review: vitamin D, bisphosphonates (if osteoporotic).  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Home safety assessment (OT/physio), mobility aids, strength/balance training.  
  - Cardiology follow-up if arrhythmia (e.g., refer for pacemaker if high-degree AV block).  
  - Osteoporosis clinic if fragility fracture.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann’s, 1 L/8h adjusted for oral intake and cardiac status – use caution in heart failure.  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin (e.g., 40 mg SC once daily) or tinzaparin – **but reassess bleeding risk with head injury or spinal trauma**; discuss with senior if active bleeding or coagulopathy.  
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if hip fracture planned for theatre; otherwise full diet with encouragement.  
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly NEWS2; neuro obs q1h if head injury or GCS ≤14.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO/IV q6h; alternatives: codeine 30–60 mg PO/IM q4-6h (PRN) or tramadol 50–100 mg PO q6h (avoid in elderly if sedation risk). NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg PO q8h, or diclofenac PR 100 mg once daily) – avoid in AKI, GI bleeding, hypovolaemia.  
- **Bone protection:** Alendronic acid 70 mg PO weekly + calcium/vitamin D supplementation (e.g. Adcal D3 1 tab BD).  
- **Anticoagulation reversal (if intracranial bleed):** Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) dose based on INR: e.g., 25–50 IU/kg IV (check local protocol). Vitamin K 5–10 mg IV slow for warfarin.  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs + ACEi/ARB → additive renal impairment.  
  - Enoxaparin + antiplatelets → increased bleeding risk.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Enoxaparin reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g., 20 mg SC once daily).  
  - Alendronic acid avoid if CrCl <35 mL/min.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Under-dosing of anticoagulation reversal** (e.g., giving only vitamin K without PCC for life-threatening bleed, or giving wrong PCC dose). Also: **Not checking renal function before enoxaparin in elderly** (risk of accumulation and bleeding).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Intracranial haematoma expansion, cerebral herniation.  
  - Spinal cord compression from fracture.  
  - Acute kidney injury (rhabdomyolysis, hypovolaemia).  
  - Hip fracture non-union, avascular necrosis.  
  - Hospital-acquired infection (pneumonia, UTI from immobility).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Loss of independence, fear of falling, social isolation.  
  - Osteoporosis progression, recurrent falls.  
  - Chronic pain (hip, head, back).  
  - Post-concussion syndrome (headache, dizziness, memory issues).
sections.Prognosis:
- Hip fracture: ~20–30% mortality at 1 year in older adults; 40% of survivors do not return to independent living.  
- Intracranial haemorrhage mortality depends on size, location, and anticoagulation status – up to 50% with subdural in elderly.  
- Most falls are single events with treatable cause; recurrence occurs in ~30% within 1 year if no targeted intervention.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform an ECG** in any patient >60 with a fall, even if they deny chest pain or loss of consciousness – it’s the most missed killer.  
- **Check anticoagulation status and time since last dose** in any head injury – ask the patient, family, pharmacy, GP, and record it.  
- **Assess for prolonged lying time** (>1-2 hours) – ask "how long were you on the floor?" – to prompt CK, renal function, and fluid resuscitation.  
- **Consider orthostatic BP** in all elderly fallers – a quick bedside test can change management (reduce or alter hypotensive drugs).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume a fall is 'mechanical'** without considering cardiac syncope, sepsis, or medication side effects – missing the cause leads to recurrence.  
- **Do not give VTE prophylaxis** (enoxaparin) without careful bleeding risk assessment in head injury or spinal fracture – a small bleed can worsen catastrophically.  
- **Do not delay CT head** in anticoagulated patient with minor head injury – the '100%' sensitivity rule: if on warfarin/DOAC, always scan even if GCS 15 and no symptoms.  
- **Do not omit neuro observations** just because patient is in a surgical bed – a delayed subdural can present hours later.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New or worsening headache, vomiting, confusion, drowsiness.  
  - Inability to weight bear on affected leg, calf pain/swelling (DVT) or chest pain (PE) if prolonged bed rest.  
  - Falls again or new weakness/numbness in limb.  
- **Follow-up:** GP within 1 week for medication review and bone health; falls clinic referral if recurrent; orthopaedic clinic if fracture managed conservatively.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / crash team:**  
  - GCS drop > 2 points from baseline (or < 13).  
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially respiratory rate > 24, oxygen saturations < 92%, systolic BP < 90 mmHg).  
  - New fixed/dilated pupil (urgent CT + neurosurgery review).  
  - Signs of spinal cord compression (paralysis, sensory level, loss of sphincter control).  
  - Lactate > 4 mmol/L (suggests hypoperfusion, possible rhabdo/syncope cause).  
  - Uncontrolled pain or inability to pass urine (consider spinal injury).  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - If head injury and on anticoagulation – call neurosurgery early even if CT normal.  
  - If hip fracture and cannot weight bear – orthogeriatrics within 24h.

rawMarkdown:
## The Patient Who Fell
**One-liner:** Common cause of hospital attendance in older adults; high risk of serious injury, syncope, and functional decline.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Intracranial haemorrhage** (subdural/extradural after head strike) — especially on anticoagulation/antiplatelets. **Single finding:** deteriorating GCS or new unilateral fixed/dilated pupil, focal neurology.  
- **Cervical spine fracture** (elderly fall from standing, especially with head strike). **Single finding:** midline neck tenderness + neurological deficit (or documented trauma + altered consciousness).  
- **Hip fracture** (frequent in osteoporotic elderly). **Single finding:** shortened, externally rotated leg + inability to weight bear.  
- **Rhabdomyolysis** (prolonged lying on floor, e.g., >2 hours). **Single finding:** dark/cola-coloured urine + raised CK + rising creatinine.  
- **Cardiac cause of fall** (arrhythmia, myocardial infarction). **Single finding:** loss of consciousness + abnormal ECG (ST changes, AV block, atrial fibrillation).

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Found on floor; unable to get up; pain in hip/back/head; confusion; loss of consciousness (or witness report of collapse); headache, neck pain.  
- **Key exam findings:** GCS; visible head injury (scalp haematoma, periorbital ecchymosis); neck tenderness/step-off; shortened/externally rotated leg; limb weakness; external signs of fracture; urine colour (dark, decreased output); postural hypotension.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Syncope (cardiac/vasovagal/orthostatic)** → loss of consciousness + normal neurological exam; cardiac syncope often with chest pain or palpitations or ECG changes.  
- **Seizure** → tongue bite, incontinence, post-ictal confusion.  
- **Stroke/TIA** → sudden focal neurology (not necessarily fall alone).  
- **Mechanical fall** (trip/slip/obstacle) → no loss of consciousness, no prodrome.  
- **Acute illness** (UTI, sepsis, MI) → fever, hypotension, raised inflammatory markers, abnormal ECG.  
- **Medication side effect** (e.g., antihypertensives, sedatives) → postural hypotension, history of recent changes.  
- **Splitter:** Was there a clear loss of consciousness? If yes → cardiac/neurological cause (ECG/post-ictal); if no → mechanical or acute illness.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - ECG (**never miss** in any fall with loss of consciousness or age >60) – rule out arrhythmia, ischaemia, QT prolongation.  
  - Capillary blood glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia).  
  - Pulse oximetry (rule out hypoxia).  
  - Postural BP (lie, 1 min, 3 min) – if orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg with symptoms).  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, CRP, CK (rhabdomyolysis if prolonged lying), coagulation screen (if on anticoagulant), troponin (if syncope/cardiac concern).  
  - Group and save if hip fracture or head injury needing surgery.  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **CT head** (if: GCS <15 or new confusion after head injury; focal neurology; on anticoagulation/antiplatelet with head strike; clinical suspicion of subdural).  
  - **XR hip/pelvis** (if pain on hip rotation, inability to weight bear, shortened/rotated leg).  
  - **C-spine CT** (if high-risk: age >65, dangerous mechanism, neck pain/tenderness, neurological symptoms, or if head CT already needed).  
- **Special tests:**  
  - If cardiac syncope suspected and ECG normal → consider echo (structural disease), 24h tape (arrhythmia), tilt table (if recurrent).  
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** in any fall with loss of consciousness – omission risks missing occult MI or arrhythmia.  
  - Also: **CT head** in anyone on anticoagulation with head strike, even if asymptomatic.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - ABCDE approach, spinal precautions if c-spine suspicion; high-flow O₂ if trauma/respiratory compromise.  
  - Reverse anticoagulation if acute intracranial bleed: prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) + vitamin K (for warfarin) – dose per local protocol; for DOACs, specific reversal agents ([check local policy]).  
  - Analgesia for injury: paracetamol 1g IV/PO (avoid NSAIDs if bleeding risk or renal impairment).  
  - Fluid resuscitation if hypotensive (crystalloid 500 mL bolus, reassess).  
  - If hip fracture: gentle traction, orthopaedic referral, NBM if surgical plan.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Falls assessment: medication review (stop unnecessary sedatives, antihypertensives), orthostatic BP, vision/hearing check, gait and balance assessment.  
  - Orthogeriatric input for hip fracture (can't weight bear, surgical candidate).  
  - Monitor neuro observations (GCS, pupil size, limb power) if head injury – frequency per local protocol (e.g., q15min for first 2h, then q30min if stable).  
  - CK and renal function monitoring if rhabdomyolysis: IV fluids (aim for urine output >2 mL/kg/hr if CK >5000 IU/L).  
  - Bone protection review: vitamin D, bisphosphonates (if osteoporotic).  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Home safety assessment (OT/physio), mobility aids, strength/balance training.  
  - Cardiology follow-up if arrhythmia (e.g., refer for pacemaker if high-degree AV block).  
  - Osteoporosis clinic if fragility fracture.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann’s, 1 L/8h adjusted for oral intake and cardiac status – use caution in heart failure.  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin (e.g., 40 mg SC once daily) or tinzaparin – **but reassess bleeding risk with head injury or spinal trauma**; discuss with senior if active bleeding or coagulopathy.  
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if hip fracture planned for theatre; otherwise full diet with encouragement.  
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly NEWS2; neuro obs q1h if head injury or GCS ≤14.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO/IV q6h; alternatives: codeine 30–60 mg PO/IM q4-6h (PRN) or tramadol 50–100 mg PO q6h (avoid in elderly if sedation risk). NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg PO q8h, or diclofenac PR 100 mg once daily) – avoid in AKI, GI bleeding, hypovolaemia.  
- **Bone protection:** Alendronic acid 70 mg PO weekly + calcium/vitamin D supplementation (e.g. Adcal D3 1 tab BD).  
- **Anticoagulation reversal (if intracranial bleed):** Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) dose based on INR: e.g., 25–50 IU/kg IV (check local protocol). Vitamin K 5–10 mg IV slow for warfarin.  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs + ACEi/ARB → additive renal impairment.  
  - Enoxaparin + antiplatelets → increased bleeding risk.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Enoxaparin reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g., 20 mg SC once daily).  
  - Alendronic acid avoid if CrCl <35 mL/min.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Under-dosing of anticoagulation reversal** (e.g., giving only vitamin K without PCC for life-threatening bleed, or giving wrong PCC dose). Also: **Not checking renal function before enoxaparin in elderly** (risk of accumulation and bleeding).

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Intracranial haematoma expansion, cerebral herniation.  
  - Spinal cord compression from fracture.  
  - Acute kidney injury (rhabdomyolysis, hypovolaemia).  
  - Hip fracture non-union, avascular necrosis.  
  - Hospital-acquired infection (pneumonia, UTI from immobility).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Loss of independence, fear of falling, social isolation.  
  - Osteoporosis progression, recurrent falls.  
  - Chronic pain (hip, head, back).  
  - Post-concussion syndrome (headache, dizziness, memory issues).

### Prognosis
- Hip fracture: ~20–30% mortality at 1 year in older adults; 40% of survivors do not return to independent living.  
- Intracranial haemorrhage mortality depends on size, location, and anticoagulation status – up to 50% with subdural in elderly.  
- Most falls are single events with treatable cause; recurrence occurs in ~30% within 1 year if no targeted intervention.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform an ECG** in any patient >60 with a fall, even if they deny chest pain or loss of consciousness – it’s the most missed killer.  
- **Check anticoagulation status and time since last dose** in any head injury – ask the patient, family, pharmacy, GP, and record it.  
- **Assess for prolonged lying time** (>1-2 hours) – ask "how long were you on the floor?" – to prompt CK, renal function, and fluid resuscitation.  
- **Consider orthostatic BP** in all elderly fallers – a quick bedside test can change management (reduce or alter hypotensive drugs).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume a fall is 'mechanical'** without considering cardiac syncope, sepsis, or medication side effects – missing the cause leads to recurrence.  
- **Do not give VTE prophylaxis** (enoxaparin) without careful bleeding risk assessment in head injury or spinal fracture – a small bleed can worsen catastrophically.  
- **Do not delay CT head** in anticoagulated patient with minor head injury – the '100%' sensitivity rule: if on warfarin/DOAC, always scan even if GCS 15 and no symptoms.  
- **Do not omit neuro observations** just because patient is in a surgical bed – a delayed subdural can present hours later.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - New or worsening headache, vomiting, confusion, drowsiness.  
  - Inability to weight bear on affected leg, calf pain/swelling (DVT) or chest pain (PE) if prolonged bed rest.  
  - Falls again or new weakness/numbness in limb.  
- **Follow-up:** GP within 1 week for medication review and bone health; falls clinic referral if recurrent; orthopaedic clinic if fracture managed conservatively.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / crash team:**  
  - GCS drop > 2 points from baseline (or < 13).  
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially respiratory rate > 24, oxygen saturations < 92%, systolic BP < 90 mmHg).  
  - New fixed/dilated pupil (urgent CT + neurosurgery review).  
  - Signs of spinal cord compression (paralysis, sensory level, loss of sphincter control).  
  - Lactate > 4 mmol/L (suggests hypoperfusion, possible rhabdo/syncope cause).  
  - Uncontrolled pain or inability to pass urine (consider spinal injury).  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - If head injury and on anticoagulation – call neurosurgery early even if CT normal.  
  - If hip fracture and cannot weight bear – orthogeriatrics within 24h.
